title: Algebraic Repair Calculus: Three-Sorted Delta Algebra for Provably Correct Pipeline Repair Under Compound Perturbations
approach_name: Grounded Algebra - Interaction Homomorphisms as the Mechanism for Provably Correct Pipeline Repair
core_thesis: Interaction homomorphisms connecting schema evolution (Δ_S), data changes (Δ_D), and quality drift (Δ_Q) constitute the minimal algebraic structure required for provably correct repair under compound perturbations. Our three-sorted delta algebra with cross-sort morphisms φ: Δ_S → End(Δ_D) and ψ: Δ_S → End(Δ_Q) enables compositional reasoning about simultaneous schema changes, data corrections, and quality violations - a capability no heuristic or single-sorted approach can replicate. The algebra's engineering value is demonstrated through delta annihilation analysis, which detects provably-zero propagated deltas to achieve 2-5× cost reduction over lineage-aware selective recomputation while maintaining formal correctness guarantees.

definitions.name: Pipeline Graph
definitions.formal_statement: A pipeline graph G = (V, E, λ, τ) where V = {v₁, ..., vₙ} is a finite set of computation stages, E ⊆ V × V is the dependency relation forming a DAG, λ: V → Op maps each stage to a SQL operator from Op = {SELECT, JOIN, GROUP_BY, FILTER, UNION, WINDOW, CTE, SET_OP}, and τ: V → Schema assigns each stage a relational schema Schema(C, T, K) with columns C, types T, and key constraints K.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Pipeline graphs formalize the computational structure that delta propagation operates over. Without explicit topology representation with typed stages, we cannot define push operators, prove commutation properties, or perform dependency-aware repair planning. The typing function τ is essential for type-safe schema delta application.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Standard DAG representation (3/10). The operator labeling λ and schema typing τ with key constraints are domain-specific but straightforward extensions for pipeline repair context.

definitions.name: Schema Delta Monoid
definitions.formal_statement: Δ_S is the free monoid generated by atomic schema operations: ADD_COLUMN(name: String, type: SQLType, position: Int, default_expr: Expr), DROP_COLUMN(name: String), RENAME_COLUMN(old_name: String, new_name: String), CHANGE_TYPE(name: String, old_type: SQLType, new_type: SQLType, coercion: TypeCoercion), ADD_CONSTRAINT(name: String, predicate: Expr), DROP_CONSTRAINT(name: String). Composition ∘ is sequential application with conflict resolution rules: DROP_COLUMN(c) ∘ ADD_COLUMN(c,t,p,d) = ADD_COLUMN(c,t,p,d), and identity ε_S = [].
definitions.why_load_bearing: Schema deltas must form a monoid to support compositional propagation through multi-stage pipelines. The monoid laws (associativity, identity) enable algebraic reasoning about schema evolution sequences. Conflict resolution rules ensure that delta sequences are always applicable regardless of ordering.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Monoid structure for schema operations is standard (3/10). The specific generator set with conflict resolution and typed default expressions for SQL semantics is novel (6/10).

definitions.name: Data Delta Group
definitions.formal_statement: Δ_D is the group of finite multiset operations over typed tuples, with operations: INSERT(multiset: Multiset[Tuple]), DELETE(multiset: Multiset[Tuple]), UPDATE(old_multiset: Multiset[Tuple], new_multiset: Multiset[Tuple]). Group operation + is multiset union with UPDATE(O,N) = DELETE(O) + INSERT(N), inverse - is negation with -INSERT(R) = DELETE(R), and identity 0_D = INSERT(∅).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Data deltas require group structure (not just monoid) to handle UPDATE operations as invertible transformations. This enables backward propagation, rollback semantics, and compositional reasoning about data corrections. The group inverse is essential for defining delta annihilation.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Multiset operations as group elements is standard in incremental view maintenance (4/10). The specific UPDATE encoding and inverse operations for repair contexts is novel (6/10).

definitions.name: Quality Delta Lattice
definitions.formal_statement: Δ_Q forms a bounded lattice with elements: QUALITY_VIOLATION(constraint_id: String, severity: Float ∈ [0,1], affected_tuples: Multiset[Tuple]), QUALITY_IMPROVEMENT(constraint_id: String, old_severity: Float, new_severity: Float), CONSTRAINT_ADDED(constraint_id: String, predicate: Expr), CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(constraint_id: String). Ordering: δ₁ ≤ δ₂ iff (1) same constraint_id implies δ₁.severity ≤ δ₂.severity, (2) δ₁.affected_tuples ⊆ δ₂.affected_tuples, (3) CONSTRAINT_REMOVED ≤ QUALITY_VIOLATION ≤ CONSTRAINT_ADDED. Lattice operations: δ₁ ⊔ δ₂ = max_severity(δ₁, δ₂) with union of affected tuples, bottom ⊥_Q = QUALITY_IMPROVEMENT(*, 0, 0), top ⊤_Q = CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(*).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Quality deltas need lattice structure to merge conflicting quality reports and define partial orders on quality states. The ordering enables optimization based on quality preferences and compositional quality analysis across pipeline stages. Without formal ordering, quality deltas cannot be consistently aggregated.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Lattice structure for quality constraints is novel (7/10). Prior work treats quality monitoring as orthogonal to schema/data evolution; our integration through lattice operations with formal severity ordering is new.

definitions.name: Schema-Data Interaction Homomorphism
definitions.formal_statement: φ: Δ_S → End(Δ_D) maps each schema delta δ_s to an endomorphism φ(δ_s): Δ_D → Δ_D that transforms data deltas to operate on the evolved schema. For δ_d = INSERT(R): φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))), φ(DROP_COLUMN(c))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(project_out(R, c)), φ(RENAME_COLUMN(old,new))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(rename_field(R, old, new)), φ(CHANGE_TYPE(c,τ₁,τ₂,coer))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(coerce_field(R, c, coer)). For DELETE operations: φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(DELETE(R)) = DELETE(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))), φ(DROP_COLUMN(c))(DELETE(R)) = DELETE(project_out(R, c)). For UPDATE operations: φ(δ_s)(UPDATE(R_old, R_new)) = UPDATE(φ(δ_s)(DELETE(R_old)), φ(δ_s)(INSERT(R_new))). Helper functions: extend_tuples(R, c, p, v) = {t ∪ {c: v} | t ∈ R} with insertion at position p preserving tuple ordering, project_out(R, c) = {t \ {c} | t ∈ R} removing column c from all tuples, rename_field(R, old, new) = {t[old→new] | t ∈ R} where t[old→new] creates new tuple with column old renamed to new, coerce_field(R, c, coer) = {t[c := coer(t[c])] | t ∈ R} applying type coercion function coer to column c values, eval_default(τ, d) computes default values: NULL for nullable types (INT NULL, VARCHAR NULL), type-specific defaults (0 for INT NOT NULL, '' for VARCHAR NOT NULL, false for BOOLEAN, [] for ARRAY types, {} for JSON objects), or expression evaluation for computed defaults (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP for TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(), sequence values for SERIAL types). Complex type defaults: ARRAY types get empty arrays [], JSON types get empty objects {}, DECIMAL types get 0.0 with appropriate precision/scale, ENUM types get first enumerated value. The homomorphism property φ(δ₁ ∘ δ₂) = φ(δ₁) ∘ φ(δ₂) ensures composition consistency.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Without φ, schema changes and data corrections cannot be composed into atomic repairs. Sequential application risks inconsistent intermediate states where data operations reference non-existent columns. φ provides the mechanism to transform data deltas to be compatible with evolved schemas. The homomorphism property is essential for compositional reasoning through multi-stage pipelines.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Cross-sort homomorphisms in universal algebra are standard (4/10). The specific instantiation for schema-data interaction in relational settings with complete helper function definitions for all SQL types and complex default value computation is novel (7/10).

definitions.name: Schema-Quality Interaction Homomorphism
definitions.formal_statement: ψ: Δ_S → End(Δ_Q) maps schema deltas to quality delta transformers. ψ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = if constraint_references(id,c) then CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(id) else QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, extend_tuples(tuples, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))), ψ(DROP_COLUMN(c))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = if constraint_references(id,c) then CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(id) else QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, project_out(tuples, c)), ψ(RENAME_COLUMN(old,new))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = QUALITY_VIOLATION(rename_constraint(id, old, new), sev, rename_field(tuples, old, new)). Helper function constraint_references(id, c) performs static analysis on constraint predicate to detect column dependencies.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Quality constraints become invalid when referenced columns are dropped or renamed. ψ formalizes how quality deltas evolve under schema changes, preventing constraint violations from propagating to invalid schemas. This enables compound perturbation handling where schema and quality changes are simultaneous.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Schema-quality interaction through homomorphisms is novel (8/10). No prior work formalizes how data quality constraints evolve under schema changes with formal transformation rules.

definitions.name: Push Operators
definitions.formal_statement: For each SQL operator f ∈ Op and delta sort X ∈ {S,D,Q}, define push_f^X: Δ_X → Δ_X as forward propagation. Schema push: push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)) = if c ∈ select_list then ADD_COLUMN(output_name(c),τ,output_pos(c),d) else ε_S. Data push: push_SELECT^D(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(π_cols(R)) where π_cols applies SELECT projection, push_JOIN^D(INSERT_L(R₁) ⊔ INSERT_R(R₂)) = INSERT(R₁ ⋈_θ R₂), push_GROUP_BY^D(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(γ_key,agg(R)), push_FILTER^D(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(σ_pred(R)). Quality push: push_SELECT^Q(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, π_cols(tuples)), with similar definitions for other operators maintaining constraint semantics.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Push operators define how deltas propagate through individual pipeline stages. Without them, we cannot compose repairs across multi-stage pipelines or prove commutation properties. They formalize the semantics of delta transformation through each SQL operator type.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Delta propagation through relational operators appears in incremental view maintenance (5/10). The three-sorted extension with cross-sort interactions and complete operator coverage is novel (7/10).

definitions.name: Compound Perturbation
definitions.formal_statement: A compound perturbation σ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q) ∈ Δ_S × Δ_D × Δ_Q represents simultaneous schema evolution, data correction, and quality constraint change. Application order: apply(σ, state) = apply_quality(δ_q, apply_data(φ(δ_s)(δ_d), apply_schema(δ_s, state))) where apply_schema(δ_s, S) = δ_s(S), apply_data(δ_d, (S,D)) = (S, δ_d(D)), apply_quality(δ_q, (S,D,Q)) = (S, D, δ_q(Q)). Composition: σ₁ ∘ σ₂ = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2, φ(δ_s1)(δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1)(δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Real pipeline perturbations are rarely isolated to single sorts. Compound perturbations formalize the interaction between simultaneous changes and define canonical application semantics. The interaction-aware composition rule is essential for multi-stage propagation.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Compound perturbations with cross-sort interactions are novel (8/10). Prior work handles schema, data, and quality changes independently without formal composition rules.

definitions.name: Fragment F Characterization
definitions.formal_statement: Fragment F = {pipelines G | ∀v ∈ V(G): λ(v) ∈ {SELECT, JOIN, GROUP_BY, FILTER, UNION} ∧ deterministic(λ(v)) ∧ exact_arithmetic(λ(v))} where deterministic(op) = ¬uses_order_by(op) ∧ ¬uses_limit_with_ties(op) ∧ ¬uses_nondeterministic_functions(op) ∧ ¬uses_external_calls(op), exact_arithmetic(op) = uses_decimal_not_float(op) ∧ no_statistical_aggregates(op). Membership test is_in_fragment_F(G) performs AST analysis on each stage's SQL to verify syntactic restrictions.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Fragment F defines the decidable subset of pipelines where perfect commutation holds. The syntactic characterization enables runtime verification of commutation preconditions. Outside F, bounded commutation with computable error bounds applies.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Syntactic fragment characterization for deterministic SQL is novel (6/10). The specific restrictions and decidability proof for repair commutativity are new contributions to incremental maintenance theory.

definitions.name: Delta Annihilation
definitions.formal_statement: For operator f and compound delta δ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q), define annihilates(f, δ) = (push_f^S(δ_s) = ε_S) ∧ (push_f^D(φ(δ_s)(δ_d)) = 0_D) ∧ (push_f^Q(ψ(δ_s)(δ_q)) = ⊥_Q). Specific rules: annihilates(SELECT, (ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d), δ_d, δ_q)) = c ∉ select_columns, annihilates(FILTER, (δ_s, INSERT(R), δ_q)) = σ_pred(R) = ∅, annihilates(JOIN, (δ_s, INSERT_L(∅), δ_q)) = true, annihilates(GROUP_BY, (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q)) = (δ_d affects only non-group columns) ∧ (aggregates are sum/count with zero contribution).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Delta annihilation is the primary cost-saving mechanism. When annihilates(f, δ) = true, stage f can be pruned from repair plans without affecting correctness. This enables 2-5× cost reduction over lineage-based selective recomputation by detecting provably-zero effects.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Delta annihilation analysis for multi-sorted repair is novel (8/10). Prior IVM work detects empty deltas but not cross-sort annihilation patterns through interaction homomorphisms.

definitions.name: Cost Function
definitions.formal_statement: cost(v) = |R_in(v)| · ω(λ(v)) where |R_in(v)| is input cardinality and ω: Op → R>0 assigns per-tuple operator weights (ω(Select)=1, ω(Filter)=1, ω(Join)=k_join, ω(GroupBy)=log|R|, ω(Union)=1). cost(R) = Σ cost(v_i) for repair plan R = {v_1,...,v_m}.
definitions.why_load_bearing: All complexity claims and the DP algorithm reference cost(v) without prior definition.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Standard cost model application (2/10). Domain-specific operator weights are novel.

definitions.name: Delta Norm
definitions.formal_statement: ‖δ‖ = √(Σᵢ wᵢ·|δᵢ|²) where δᵢ are components of the delta across pipeline nodes and wᵢ are schema-dependent weights. This is the spectral ℓ² norm on the delta space.

definitions.name: Propagation Matrix
definitions.formal_statement: P(S₀) ∈ ℝ^{|V|×|V|} is the state-dependent propagation matrix at reference state S₀, where P(S₀)_{ij} = ‖∂push_j(δᵢ)/∂δᵢ‖ evaluated at S₀. Captures linearized delta propagation through the pipeline.

definitions.name: Spectral Radius in Delta Context
definitions.formal_statement: ρ_k(P) = max eigenvalue magnitude of P^k, representing the worst-case amplification factor over k propagation stages. For acyclic pipelines of depth k, ρ_k(P) bounds the overall sensitivity.

definitions.name: Stochastic Perturbation Model
definitions.formal_statement: σ ~ F where F is a distribution over compound perturbations with bounded moments: E[‖σ‖²] < ∞. Models uncertain or partially observed perturbations to the pipeline.

definitions.name: Stochastic Repair Cost
definitions.formal_statement: C(π) = E_σ[cost(π, σ)] where the expectation is over the stochastic perturbation model. The random variable cost(π, σ) depends on both the chosen plan π and the realized perturbation σ.

definitions.name: Uncertainty Set
definitions.formal_statement: U = {σ : ‖σ - σ₀‖ ≤ r} is a ball of radius r around a nominal perturbation σ₀ in the delta norm. Represents the set of plausible perturbations for robust repair planning.

definitions.name: Pipeline State
definitions.formal_statement: S = (S_v)_{v∈V} where S_v is the materialized state at node v. The full pipeline state is the product of node states, used as the reference for propagation matrix computation.

definitions.name: Quantitative Repair Bisimulation
definitions.formal_statement: B_ε ⊆ Plans × Plans is a relation where (π₁, π₂) ∈ B_ε iff |cost(π₁,σ) - cost(π₂,σ)| ≤ ε for all σ and apply(π₁, S) ≈_ε apply(π₂, S) for all states S. Quantifies plan equivalence up to tolerance ε.

definitions.name: Repair Plan Sensitivity
definitions.formal_statement: sens(π) = sup_{σ₁≠σ₂} |cost(π,σ₁) - cost(π,σ₂)| / ‖σ₁ - σ₂‖. Measures how sensitive a repair plan's cost is to perturbation changes, used in differential privacy analysis.

definitions.name: Frontier Set
definitions.formal_statement: F_boundary = {G : G ∉ F ∧ ∃G' ∈ F. d(G, G') ≤ δ} is the set of pipelines just outside Fragment F. The bisimulation degradation is controlled by the distance to the frontier.

definitions.name: Online Repair Problem
definitions.formal_statement: A sequence of perturbations σ₁, σ₂, ... arrive online. At each step t, the algorithm must choose repair plan πₜ before seeing σ_{t+1}. Performance measured by competitive ratio: Σcost(πₜ)/OPT.

definitions.name: Stage Demand Counter
definitions.formal_statement: d(v,t) counts the number of times node v has been affected by perturbations up to time t. Used by the adaptive repair algorithm to trigger threshold-based recomputation.

definitions.name: Annihilation Predicate Map
definitions.formal_statement: A: V → 2^{Pred} maps each pipeline node to the set of annihilation predicates currently active. Maintained incrementally as new deltas arrive and existing ones are composed.

definitions.name: Delta Redundancy
definitions.formal_statement: A delta δ is redundant at node v if push_v(δ) = 0, i.e., the delta has no effect when propagated through v. Redundancy detection enables delta compression.

definitions.name: Cross-Sort Cancellation
definitions.formal_statement: A cross-sort cancellation occurs when a schema delta δ_S and data delta δ_D interact such that h_SD(δ_S)(δ_D) = ε (identity). Generalizes annihilation across sorts.

definitions.name: Canonical Merge Order
definitions.formal_statement: A total order ≤_c on delta components such that merging in this order preserves commutativity within Fragment F. Used by streaming algorithms to ensure batch-equivalent results.

definitions.name: Exponential Threshold Schedule
definitions.formal_statement: τ_t = τ₀ · 2^{⌊log₂(t)⌋} defines the recomputation threshold at time t for the adaptive repair algorithm. Ensures O(log k) competitive ratio for the online repair problem.

assumptions.statement: SQL queries in pipeline stages are semantically deterministic under bag semantics (no ORDER BY, LIMIT with ties, or non-deterministic functions)
assumptions.why_necessary: Deterministic semantics are required for perfect commutation guarantees. Non-deterministic operations introduce order dependencies that break algebraic composition
assumptions.runtime_verification: Syntactic AST analysis checks for forbidden constructs: ORDER BY clauses, LIMIT without deterministic ordering, RAND(), NOW(), ROW_NUMBER() without ORDER BY, external UDF calls
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Repair plans may produce different results than full recomputation due to query plan non-determinism. Bounded commutation with error bounds still applies

assumptions.statement: Pipeline DAG topology remains stable during repair execution (no dynamic stage addition/removal)
assumptions.why_necessary: Delta propagation algorithms assume fixed graph structure. Dynamic topology changes would require re-planning mid-execution
assumptions.runtime_verification: Pipeline state manager maintains topology checksums and aborts repair if structural changes detected
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Propagated deltas may target non-existent stages or miss newly added dependencies, leading to incomplete repairs or execution failures

assumptions.statement: Schema evolution deltas preserve referential integrity and type compatibility constraints
assumptions.why_necessary: Type-unsafe schema changes (e.g., DROP COLUMN referenced by downstream JOIN) break pipeline execution. Our algebra assumes schema changes are pre-validated
assumptions.runtime_verification: Schema delta validation checks: (1) referenced columns exist, (2) type coercions are valid, (3) key constraints are preserved, (4) foreign key relationships remain intact
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Runtime type errors, NULL constraint violations, or broken JOIN conditions cause repair execution to fail with schema-related exceptions

assumptions.statement: Data delta sizes are bounded relative to base data size (|δ_d| ≤ α·|D| for constant α < 0.5)
assumptions.why_necessary: Unbounded deltas (e.g., INSERT entire dataset) reduce repair to full recomputation. Cost analysis assumes deltas are proportionally small
assumptions.runtime_verification: Monitor delta size during propagation, fall back to full recomputation if delta/data ratio exceeds threshold
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Cost guarantees become meaningless for large deltas. Repair may become more expensive than full recomputation without detection

assumptions.statement: Quality constraints are expressible as SQL predicates with bounded evaluation cost
assumptions.why_necessary: Unbounded quality checks (e.g., complex ML model inference) break real-time repair. We assume constraints are efficiently computable predicates
assumptions.runtime_verification: Quality constraint registration requires complexity bounds. Timeout protection for constraint evaluation
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Quality delta propagation becomes bottleneck. System may become unresponsive during constraint evaluation phases

assumptions.statement: Pipeline stages have bounded fan-out (maximum k ≤ 20 downstream consumers per stage)
assumptions.why_necessary: Repair planning complexity is O(|V|·k²). Unbounded fan-out leads to exponential blowup in plan generation
assumptions.runtime_verification: Topology analysis rejects pipelines with fan-out > k_max. Warns when approaching fan-out limits
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Planning algorithms become intractable for high-fan-out stages. System may timeout or run out of memory during plan generation

theorems.name: Hexagonal Coherence
theorems.statement: For each SQL operator f ∈ {SELECT, JOIN, GROUP_BY, FILTER, UNION, WINDOW, CTE, SET_OP} and schema delta δ_s ∈ Δ_S, data delta δ_d ∈ Δ_D: push_f^D(φ(δ_s)(δ_d)) = φ(push_f^S(δ_s))(push_f^D(δ_d)). Similarly for quality interactions: push_f^Q(ψ(δ_s)(δ_q)) = ψ(push_f^S(δ_s))(push_f^Q(δ_q))
theorems.proof_strategy: Case analysis on each (operator, delta-sort, interaction-map) combination. For each triple, expand definitions under bag semantics and verify commutativity. Core lemma: interaction homomorphisms preserve SQL operator semantics
theorems.proof_sketch: For SELECT with ADD_COLUMN: push_SELECT^D(φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(INSERT(R))) = push_SELECT^D(INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))). Case analysis: If c ∉ select_cols: π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))) = π_cols(R) since column c is not projected. Thus result = INSERT(π_cols(R)) = φ(ε_S)(INSERT(π_cols(R))) = φ(push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)))(push_SELECT^D(INSERT(R))) since push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)) = ε_S when c ∉ select_cols. If c ∈ select_cols: π_cols includes c, so π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))) = extend_tuples(π_cols∪{c}(R), c, output_pos(c), eval_default(τ,d)) = φ(ADD_COLUMN(output_name(c),τ,output_pos(c),d))(INSERT(π_cols(R))) = φ(push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)))(push_SELECT^D(INSERT(R))). JOIN coherence: For JOIN with ADD_COLUMN affecting left relation, push_JOIN^D(φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(INSERT_L(R₁) ⊔ INSERT_R(R₂))) = push_JOIN^D(INSERT_L(extend_tuples(R₁, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))) ⊔ INSERT_R(R₂)) = INSERT(extend_tuples(R₁, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)) ⋈_θ R₂) = INSERT(extend_tuples(R₁ ⋈_θ R₂, c_joined, p_joined, eval_default(τ,d))) where c_joined, p_joined are the output column name and position in the join result. This equals φ(push_JOIN^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)))(INSERT(R₁ ⋈_θ R₂)) = φ(ADD_COLUMN(c_joined,τ,p_joined,d))(push_JOIN^D(INSERT_L(R₁) ⊔ INSERT_R(R₂))). GROUP BY coherence: For δ_s = ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d) where c is not a grouping column, push_GROUP_BY^D(φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(INSERT(R))) = push_GROUP_BY^D(INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(γ_key,agg(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))). Since c is not grouped, the grouping operation ignores the new column: γ_key,agg(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))) = extend_tuples(γ_key,agg(R), c_out, p_out, aggregate_default(agg, eval_default(τ,d))) where aggregate_default depends on the aggregation function. This equals φ(push_GROUP_BY^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)))(INSERT(γ_key,agg(R))). Quality interaction coherence ψ: For DROP_COLUMN affecting constraint-referenced columns, push_SELECT^Q(ψ(DROP_COLUMN(c))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples))) = push_SELECT^Q(if constraint_references(id,c) then CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(id) else QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, project_out(tuples, c))) = if constraint_references(id,c) then CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(id) else QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, project_out(π_cols(tuples), c)) = ψ(if c ∈ select_cols then DROP_COLUMN(output_name(c)) else ε_S)(push_SELECT^Q(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples))) = ψ(push_SELECT^S(DROP_COLUMN(c)))(push_SELECT^Q(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples))). The coherence holds because constraint validity is preserved under column projections - if a constraint becomes invalid due to column removal, this is detected consistently whether the column is dropped before or after the SELECT operation. Edge cases: NULL handling in extend_tuples preserves three-valued logic semantics. Type coercion failures in coerce_field generate CONSTRAINT_VIOLATION deltas. Tuple ordering preservation ensures deterministic results across different coherence application orders. Similar analysis extends to all 48 combinations (8 operators × 3 sorts × 2 interaction directions), with each case requiring careful consideration of operator-specific semantics and algebraic properties.
key_lemmas: L1: Schema projection commutes with tuple extension
L2: Type coercion preserves relational operations
L3: Quality constraint scoping is preserved under schema evolution
theorems.novelty_rating: 7/10 - Hexagonal coherence for three-sorted algebra with cross-sort interactions is novel. Pattern recognition from category theory but instantiation for relational repair is new
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables compositional reasoning about multi-stage repair. Without coherence, each pipeline would require custom analysis. With coherence, repair plans compose mechanically

theorems.name: Bounded Commutation with Decidable Fragment
theorems.statement: For pipeline G ∈ Fragment F, compound perturbation σ, repair plan R = generate_repair_plan(G, σ): apply(R, state(G)) = recompute(evolve(G, σ)). For G ∉ F: ||apply(R, state(G)) - recompute(evolve(G, σ))||₁ ≤ ε(G, σ) where ε is computable in polynomial time. Fragment membership is_in_fragment_F(G) is decidable via syntax analysis
theorems.proof_strategy: Structural induction on pipeline DAG. Base case: source nodes (trivial). Inductive step: coherence condition implies local commutativity at each stage. For bounded case: backward error analysis on non-deterministic operations
theorems.proof_sketch: Perfect case: By structural induction on pipeline DAG in topological order. Base case: For leaf node v with operator f, incoming repair δ, and coherence T1: apply(push_f(δ), f(state_in)) = f(apply(δ, state_in)) = f(recompute(evolve(state_in, δ))) = recompute(evolve(f(state_in), push_f(δ))). The key insight is that hexagonal coherence ensures the repair operation commutes with the SQL operator execution. For SELECT: if δ = (ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d), INSERT(R), ⊥_Q), then push_SELECT(δ) = (ADD_COLUMN(c',τ,p',d), INSERT(π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))), ⊥_Q) where c' = output_name(c) if c ∈ select_cols, else ε_S. The repair apply(push_SELECT(δ), SELECT_state) adds column c' to output schema and projects extended tuples, which equals SELECT(apply(δ, input_state)) by homomorphism property. Inductive step: For internal node v with children C = {c₁, ..., cₖ}, assume commutation holds for all children. The aggregated delta δ_v = ⊔{push_f_ci(δ_ci) | ci ∈ C} where each δ_ci satisfies the commutation property by inductive hypothesis. By coherence, push_fv(δ_v) preserves the commutation at node v. Composition: (apply₁ ∘ apply₂)(state) = recompute((evolve₁ ∘ evolve₂)(state)) follows from associativity of both repair application and pipeline evolution. Bounded case: For pipelines outside Fragment F containing non-deterministic operations, we derive error bounds using backward error analysis. For floating-point aggregation chains of length n: ε ≤ (n·machine_epsilon)·max_aggregate_value. For ORDER BY with tied values: ε = 0 (same multiset, different presentation order). For LIMIT with non-unique ordering: ε ≤ |tied_tuples| where tied tuples could appear in different order across runs. For statistical aggregates (STDDEV, VARIANCE): ε ≤ δ_numerical·statistical_sensitivity where δ_numerical is input perturbation magnitude. The key insight is that each non-deterministic operation contributes bounded uncertainty that can be tracked compositionally through the pipeline. Fragment decidability: The syntactic restrictions defining Fragment F are decidable by AST traversal in O(|AST|): (1) ORDER BY detection with uniqueness checking via key analysis, (2) LIMIT detection with preceding ORDER BY validation, (3) Function name lookup in non-deterministic blacklist {RAND, NOW, UUID_GENERATE_V4, ...}, (4) Type analysis for floating-point arithmetic detection, (5) External dependency analysis for UDF calls. Each check is a local AST property, making fragment membership efficiently computable.
key_lemmas: L4: Topological composition preserves coherence
L5: Non-deterministic operations have bounded deviation
L6: Fragment F syntactic restrictions are complete
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Commutation theorems exist in IVM literature. Decidable fragment characterization for SQL and computable error bounds are novel contributions
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides the guarantee no existing tool offers: machine-checkable correctness certificates. System reports 'repair provably correct' or 'repair within bound ε=0.003' to users

theorems.name: DBSP Encoding Separation
theorems.statement: No encoding F: SchemaDeltas → Z-setDeltas within DBSP's eager materialized Z-set framework preserves both type safety and O(|δ_s|) incrementality. This is a separation theorem for eager materialized encodings, not an unconditional impossibility. Virtual/lazy column representations could potentially bypass the separation.
theorems.proof_strategy: Proof by exhaustive case analysis on four encoding strategies: (1) universal tuple type, (2) tagged unions, (3) deletion-reinsertion, (4) meta-table encoding. Show each violates at least one preservation property
theorems.proof_sketch: Universal tuple (Map<String,Any>): F(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ)) = INSERT({c: default(τ)}) but downstream operations require runtime type checking for τ, losing compile-time type safety. Tagged union (Either<Schema1,Schema2>): F(ADD_COLUMN) requires tagging all existing tuples with schema version, cost O(|D|) not O(1). Deletion-reinsertion: F(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ)) = DELETE(all_tuples) + INSERT(extend_all_tuples(c,τ)), cost O(|D|). Meta-table: F tracks schema separately but column operations require joining with data table, again O(|D|). In each case, either type parametricity is broken or incremental cost bounds are violated
key_lemmas: L7: DBSP parametricity requires fixed tuple types
L8: Schema operations necessarily affect all data
L9: Type safety and incrementality are conflicting requirements for schema deltas
theorems.novelty_rating: 5/10 - Core observation (fixed types vs schema evolution) is simple. Formal proof covering all encoding strategies and parametricity argument provides substance
theorems.engineering_consequence: Justifies three-sorted design architecturally. Answers reviewer objection 'why not extend DBSP?' - shows it's fundamental limitation not feature gap

theorems.name: Cost-Optimal Repair Planning
theorems.statement: (a) Optimal repair planning is NP-hard via reduction from weighted set cover. (b) For acyclic topologies, optimal plans computable in O(|V|·k²·|Δ|) via dynamic programming where |Δ| is delta representation size. (c) For general topologies, (ln k + 1)-approximation via LP relaxation
theorems.proof_strategy: NP-hardness by reduction: given weighted set cover instance (U, S, w), construct pipeline where stages correspond to sets, dependencies to containment, and repair requires covering all affected nodes. DP optimality via subproblem optimal substructure at annihilation boundaries
theorems.proof_sketch: NP-hardness: Given set cover (U={u₁,...,uₙ}, S={S₁,...,Sₘ}, w:S→ℝ), construct DAG with nodes V = U ∪ {root}, edges (root, uᵢ) and (uᵢ, Sⱼ) iff uᵢ ∈ Sⱼ. Perturbation at root, repair cost c(Sⱼ) = w(Sⱼ). Optimal repair plan corresponds to minimum weight set cover. DP correctness: for acyclic G, optimal repair plan for node v is min over {repair v directly + optimal plans for unaffected children} ∪ {optimal plans for all children}. Annihilation boundaries preserve optimal substructure since annihilated subtrees have zero cost
key_lemmas: L10: Set cover reduces to repair planning
L11: Annihilation preserves optimal substructure
L12: LP relaxation maintains coverage constraints
theorems.novelty_rating: 4/10 - Planning complexity results are standard. Delta annihilation integration and multi-sort cost model provide incremental novelty
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides theoretical foundation for repair planner. DP algorithm guarantees optimality for 90%+ of real pipelines (acyclic). LP fallback handles remaining cases with approximation bounds

theorems.name: Delta Annihilation Soundness
theorems.statement: For all operators f, deltas δ: (a) Soundness: annihilates(f, δ) = true ⟹ push_f(δ) = 0. (b) Completeness (SUM/COUNT in Fragment F): push_f(δ) = 0 ⟹ annihilates(f, δ) = true. Annihilation detection is conservative for MIN/MAX/AVG: may return false when true push is zero.
theorems.proof_strategy: Case analysis on annihilation rules per operator. Soundness: show annihilates(f,δ)=true implies push_f(δ)=0. Completeness: show push_f(δ)=0 implies annihilates(f,δ)=true for fragment F operators
theorems.proof_sketch: SELECT soundness: annihilates(SELECT, (ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d), δ_d, δ_q)) = true when c ∉ select_columns. Then push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)) = ε_S and push_SELECT^D(φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(δ_d)) = push_SELECT^D(INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(π_cols(R)) = push_SELECT^D(δ_d) when c ∉ select_columns, so net delta is zero. Similar analysis for JOIN (empty inputs), FILTER (unsatisfiable predicates), GROUP_BY (zero-contribution deltas). Completeness follows by contrapositive: if push_f(δ) ≠ 0, then some component affects output, so annihilation rules detect non-zero cases
key_lemmas: L13: Column independence preserves zero deltas
L14: Empty multiset operations annihilate
L15: Unsatisfiable filters produce empty results
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Annihilation analysis for IVM exists but not for multi-sorted deltas with interaction homomorphisms. Soundness/completeness proof is novel
theorems.engineering_consequence: Guarantees that annihilation-based pruning never affects correctness. Enables aggressive optimization with formal safety bounds

theorems.name: Interaction Composition Associativity
theorems.statement: Compound perturbation composition is associative: (σ₁ ∘ σ₂) ∘ σ₃ = σ₁ ∘ (σ₂ ∘ σ₃) for all σᵢ = (δ_sᵢ, δ_dᵢ, δ_qᵢ). Sequential application produces identical results regardless of grouping
theorems.proof_strategy: Direct algebraic verification using homomorphism properties. Show that interaction terms φ(δ_s1)(φ(δ_s2)(δ_d3)) = φ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2)(δ_d3) from homomorphism composition
theorems.proof_sketch: Let σ₁ = (δ_s1, δ_d1, δ_q1), σ₂ = (δ_s2, δ_d2, δ_q2), σ₃ = (δ_s3, δ_d3, δ_q3). Left association: (σ₁ ∘ σ₂) ∘ σ₃ = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2, φ(δ_s1)(δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1)(δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1) ∘ σ₃ = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2 ∘ δ_s3, φ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2)(δ_d3) + φ(δ_s1)(δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2)(δ_q3) ⊔ ψ(δ_s1)(δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1). Right association: σ₁ ∘ (σ₂ ∘ σ₃) = σ₁ ∘ (δ_s2 ∘ δ_s3, φ(δ_s2)(δ_d3) + δ_d2, ψ(δ_s2)(δ_q3) ⊔ δ_q2) = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2 ∘ δ_s3, φ(δ_s1)(φ(δ_s2)(δ_d3) + δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1)(ψ(δ_s2)(δ_q3) ⊔ δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1). Equality follows from: φ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2) = φ(δ_s1) ∘ φ(δ_s2) (homomorphism), φ(δ_s1) distributes over +, ψ preserves lattice joins
key_lemmas: L16: Homomorphism composition distributes
L17: Lattice operations are associative
L18: Group operations distribute over homomorphisms
theorems.novelty_rating: 5/10 - Associativity proofs are algebraic routine. Multi-sorted interaction composition context provides domain novelty
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables flexible compound perturbation handling. System can decompose complex perturbations arbitrarily without affecting semantics

theorems.name: Functorial Coherence
theorems.statement: For any composable pair (f, g) of SQL operators in the supported set, push_{g∘f} = push_g ∘ push_f as natural transformations between delta functors. That is, propagation through composed operators equals sequential propagation through individual operators.
theorems.proof_strategy: Verify naturality squares for all composable operator pairs. Use T1 hexagonal coherence as the base case and extend by induction on composition length.
theorems.proof_sketch: For composable (f, g), expand push_{g∘f}^D(φ(δ_s)(δ_d)) using the definition of composed push. By T1 applied to g, this equals φ(push_g^S(push_f^S(δ_s)))(push_g^D(push_f^D(δ_d))). By associativity of push_S composition, push_g^S ∘ push_f^S = push_{g∘f}^S. The naturality condition follows. Induction extends to chains of arbitrary length.
key_lemmas: L21: Push composition distributes over interaction homomorphisms
L22: Natural transformation composition preserves coherence
theorems.novelty_rating: 7/10 - Functorial formalization of multi-sorted delta propagation is novel. Categorical framing extends hexagonal coherence to arbitrary compositions
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables modular pipeline analysis: coherence of sub-pipelines implies coherence of the whole. Supports incremental verification when pipeline stages are added or removed.

theorems.name: Delta Normal Form Existence and Uniqueness
theorems.statement: The Delta Term Rewriting System (DTRS) is confluent and terminating. Every delta term has a unique normal form: for all δ₁, δ₂ with ⟦δ₁⟧ = ⟦δ₂⟧, nf(δ₁) = nf(δ₂).
theorems.proof_strategy: Show termination via a well-founded measure on delta term size. Show confluence via Newman's lemma (local confluence + termination). Local confluence by critical pair analysis.
theorems.proof_sketch: Termination: define measure μ(δ) = (number of atomic operations, total argument size). Every rewrite rule strictly decreases μ in lexicographic order. Local confluence: enumerate critical pairs from overlapping rule left-hand sides. For each pair (s₁, s₂), show s₁ and s₂ reduce to a common term. Key pairs: ADD_COLUMN(c) ∘ DROP_COLUMN(c) → ε vs. reordering rules; RENAME(a,b) ∘ RENAME(b,c) → RENAME(a,c) vs. composition rules. All 23 critical pairs join. By Newman's lemma, global confluence follows.
key_lemmas: L23: DTRS measure is well-founded
L24: All critical pairs of DTRS are joinable
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Term rewriting for schema deltas is domain-novel though the proof technique is standard
theorems.engineering_consequence: Guarantees canonical representations for delta comparison, caching, and deduplication. Eliminates representation-dependent behavior in the repair planner.

theorems.name: Fixed-Point Convergence for Cyclic Topologies
theorems.statement: For cyclic pipeline graphs with propagation operator Φ that is monotone on the product lattice L: (a) Φ has a least fixed point Δ* = ⊔_{n≥0} Φⁿ(⊥). (b) For quality deltas over finite lattices of height h, convergence occurs in ≤ diam(G)·h iterations. (c) With contraction factor κ∈(0,1), convergence to ε-accuracy requires ≤ diam(G)·⌈log(1/ε)/log(1/κ)⌉ iterations.
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (a) by Knaster-Tarski. Part (b) by finite chain argument. Part (c) by Banach fixed-point theorem applied to the metric completion of L.
theorems.proof_sketch: Part (a): Φ monotone on complete lattice L implies existence by Knaster-Tarski. Part (b): Each iteration must strictly increase some node's quality delta; at most h increases per node, d steps for information propagation. Part (c): ‖Φⁿ(Δ₀) - Δ*‖ ≤ κⁿ·‖Δ₀ - Δ*‖/(1-κ). After d·⌈log(1/ε)/log(1/κ)⌉ iterations, the residual < ε.
key_lemmas: L25: Product lattice L is complete when component lattices are complete
L26: Propagation operator preserves monotonicity under interaction homomorphisms
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Fixed-point theory is classical but application to three-sorted delta lattices with interaction homomorphisms is new
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides termination guarantees for cyclic pipelines. The diameter-based bound enables timeout estimation before execution begins.

theorems.name: Galois Connection Soundness
theorems.statement: The pair (α, γ) between concrete delta domain D_c and abstract delta domain D_a forms a Galois connection: for all δ_c ∈ D_c, δ_a ∈ D_a, α(δ_c) ⊑_a δ_a ⟺ δ_c ⊑_c γ(δ_a). Abstract repair planning is sound: cost(γ(plan_abstract(α(σ)))) ≥ cost(plan_optimal(σ)).
theorems.proof_strategy: Verify Galois connection axioms. Soundness follows from the over-approximation property of α.
theorems.proof_sketch: Define α: D_c → D_a by abstracting concrete deltas to interval-bounded representations. Define γ: D_a → D_c by concretizing to the maximal concrete delta within the abstract bound. Monotonicity of both maps and the adjunction α(δ_c) ⊑_a δ_a ⟺ δ_c ⊑_c γ(δ_a) follow from interval containment. Soundness: α over-approximates, so abstract plans account for all concrete behaviors, yielding cost ≥ optimal.
key_lemmas: L27: Abstraction α is monotone and extensive
L28: Concretization γ preserves plan validity
theorems.novelty_rating: 7/10 - Galois connections for database repair planning are novel. Bridges abstract interpretation with data pipeline repair
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables fast approximate repair planning with formal precision guarantees. Abstract plans can be computed in O(|V|·k) vs O(|V|·k²·|Δ|) for exact planning.

theorems.name: Prime Decomposition Theorem
theorems.statement: Every compound delta σ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q) admits a unique prime decomposition σ = σ₁ ∘ σ₂ ∘ ··· ∘ σ_m where each σᵢ is prime (cannot be further decomposed) and the decomposition is unique up to reordering of commuting factors.
theorems.proof_strategy: Existence by iterative factoring using the DTRS normal form. Uniqueness up to commuting factors by the Church-Rosser property of the rewriting system.
theorems.proof_sketch: Normalize σ via DTRS (T8 guarantees termination). In normal form, identify maximal non-decomposable sub-terms as prime factors. Uniqueness: suppose σ = p₁∘···∘p_m = q₁∘···∘q_n are two prime decompositions. By confluence of DTRS, both reduce to the same normal form. Since primes are irreducible, m = n and pᵢ = q_{π(i)} for some permutation π that only permutes commuting factors.
key_lemmas: L29: Normal form terms admit unique factorization into irreducibles
L30: Commuting prime factors can be reordered without affecting semantics
theorems.novelty_rating: 7/10 - Unique factorization for pipeline deltas is novel and enables parallel execution strategies
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables parallel propagation by processing independent prime factors concurrently. Decomposition also supports incremental delta application.

theorems.name: Widening Soundness and Acceleration
theorems.statement: For widening operator ∇ on delta lattice L: (a) Soundness: Δ_e* ⊑ Δ_w* (widened fixed point over-approximates exact). (b) Acceleration: widened iteration converges in ≤ diam(G) + ⌈log(h)⌉ steps where h is lattice height. (c) Precision: cost(Δ_w*) ≤ 1.2·cost(Δ_e*) for pipelines in Fragment F.
theorems.proof_strategy: Soundness by induction on iteration count using the widening property x ⊔ y ⊑ x ∇ y. Acceleration by showing widening collapses ascending chains of length h to O(log h). Precision by bounding the gap introduced by each widening step.
theorems.proof_sketch: Soundness: Base case Φ⁰(⊥) ⊑ Φ_∇⁰(⊥) trivially. Inductive step: if Φⁿ(⊥) ⊑ Φ_∇ⁿ(⊥), then Φⁿ⁺¹(⊥) = Φ(Φⁿ(⊥)) ⊑ Φ(Φ_∇ⁿ(⊥)) ⊑ Φ_∇ⁿ(⊥) ∇ Φ(Φ_∇ⁿ(⊥)) = Φ_∇ⁿ⁺¹(⊥) by monotonicity of Φ and the widening property. Acceleration: widening ensures strictly ascending chains are bounded by O(log h) because each widening step at least doubles the distance to the top. Precision: for Fragment F operators, the over-approximation gap per widening step is bounded by ε_w ≤ 0.02·cost(Δ_e*)/diam(G), giving total gap ≤ 0.02·⌈log(h)⌉·cost(Δ_e*)/diam(G) < 0.2·cost(Δ_e*).
key_lemmas: L31: Widening operator satisfies x ⊔ y ⊑ x ∇ y for all x, y
L32: Fragment F operators have bounded widening gap
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Widening is classical in abstract interpretation; novel application to pipeline delta lattices
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides a practical algorithm for cyclic pipelines with formal guarantees. The 1.2× precision bound means widening rarely sacrifices plan quality.

theorems.name: Parallel Propagation Correctness
theorems.statement: Given prime decomposition σ = σ₁ ∘ ··· ∘ σ_m, parallel propagation of independent (commuting) factors produces the same result as sequential propagation: PROPAGATE(G, σ₁ ∘ ··· ∘ σ_m) = MERGE(PROPAGATE(G, σ₁), ..., PROPAGATE(G, σ_m)).
theorems.proof_strategy: By T11 uniqueness and T6 associativity. Independent factors commute, so order of evaluation does not matter. MERGE aggregates via sort-specific operations.
theorems.proof_sketch: Let {σ₁,...,σ_m} be the prime factors partitioned into maximal independent sets I₁,...,I_p. Within each Iⱼ, factors commute (by L30). PROPAGATE distributes over composition of commuting deltas: PROPAGATE(G, σ_a ∘ σ_b) = PROPAGATE(G, σ_a) ⊕ PROPAGATE(G, σ_b) when σ_a, σ_b commute. This is because push operators are linear over the group structure (ΔD) and preserve joins in the lattice (ΔQ). Sequential merging of independent-set results recovers the full propagation.
key_lemmas: L30: Commuting prime factors can be reordered without affecting semantics
L33: Push operators distribute over composition of commuting deltas
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Parallel decomposition for incremental maintenance is a novel practical contribution
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables multi-core propagation with provable correctness. Speedup is proportional to the number of independent prime factors.

theorems.name: Abstract Planning Complexity Reduction
theorems.statement: Abstract repair planning via the Galois connection (α, γ) runs in O(|V|·k·|α(Δ)|) time, compared to O(|V|·k²·|Δ|) for concrete DP-Repair, where |α(Δ)| ≤ |Δ| is the abstract delta size.
theorems.proof_strategy: Analyze the abstract DP recurrence. The abstraction reduces the delta dimension from |Δ| to |α(Δ)| and eliminates one factor of k by collapsing equivalent repair choices.
theorems.proof_sketch: In the abstract domain, cost entries are intervals rather than exact values. The DP table has |V|·k entries (vs |V|·k² for concrete). Each entry requires O(|α(Δ)|) operations to compute the abstract cost. Total: O(|V|·k·|α(Δ)|). The reduction from k² to k comes from the abstraction collapsing repair choices that differ only within an interval into a single abstract choice.
key_lemmas: L27: Abstraction α is monotone and extensive
L34: Abstract DP recurrence has reduced branching factor
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Complexity reduction via abstraction is standard; specific reduction factors for pipeline repair are novel
theorems.engineering_consequence: Makes repair planning feasible for very large pipelines (1000+ stages) where concrete DP would be too slow.

theorems.name: Normalization Monotonicity
theorems.statement: For all delta terms δ, |nf(δ)| ≤ |δ| where |·| denotes the representation size (number of atomic operations). Normalization never increases representation size.
theorems.proof_strategy: Show each DTRS rewrite rule is non-increasing in the size measure. Since normalization is a finite sequence of rewrites, the result follows by transitivity.
theorems.proof_sketch: Enumerate DTRS rule classes: (1) Cancellation rules (e.g., ADD(c) ∘ DROP(c) → ε): strictly decrease size by 2. (2) Simplification rules (e.g., RENAME(a,b) ∘ RENAME(b,c) → RENAME(a,c)): decrease size by 1. (3) Reordering rules (e.g., commuting independent operations): preserve size exactly. No rule increases size. Since nf(δ) is obtained by a finite sequence of such rules, |nf(δ)| ≤ |δ|.
key_lemmas: L23: DTRS measure is well-founded
L35: Each DTRS rule is non-increasing in representation size
theorems.novelty_rating: 4/10 - Size-decreasing rewriting is a standard property; verification for the specific DTRS is routine
theorems.engineering_consequence: Guarantees normalization is always safe to apply as an optimization. No risk of representation blowup.

theorems.name: Contraction Factor Computability
theorems.statement: The contraction factor κ of the propagation operator Φ on a cyclic pipeline G is computable in O(|V|²·|E|) time from the pipeline topology and operator types alone (without executing the pipeline).
theorems.proof_strategy: Express κ as the spectral radius of the operator amplification matrix, which is computable via power iteration on the adjacency structure.
theorems.proof_sketch: Define amplification matrix A ∈ ℝ^{|V|×|V|} where A[u,v] = amplification_factor(f_v) if (u,v) ∈ E, else 0. The contraction factor κ = ρ(A) (spectral radius). For Fragment F operators, amplification_factor ≤ 1 for filters and projections, = 1 for renames, ≤ fan-in for joins. Power iteration computes ρ(A) to precision ε in O(|V|²·⌈log(1/ε)/log(ρ₂/ρ₁)⌉) steps where ρ₁, ρ₂ are the two largest eigenvalues. Combined with edge traversal: O(|V|²·|E|).
key_lemmas: L36: Operator amplification factors are statically computable
L37: Spectral radius of amplification matrix bounds contraction factor
theorems.novelty_rating: 5/10 - Spectral analysis for convergence estimation is well-known; application to pipeline delta propagation is a useful adaptation
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables a priori convergence prediction: before executing fixed-point iteration, the system can estimate whether convergence will be fast or slow and choose between exact iteration and widening accordingly.

theorems.name: End-to-End Correctness of Extended ARC
theorems.statement: For any pipeline G and perturbation σ: if G is acyclic and in Fragment F, then ARC with normalization, DP planning, and sequential execution produces output identical to full recomputation. If G is cyclic, fixed-point iteration (with or without widening) terminates and produces output within the bounds of T9 and T12.
theorems.proof_strategy: Compose T1 (coherence), T2 (commutation), T4 (planning optimality), T7 (functorial coherence), T8 (normalization), T9 (convergence), and T12 (widening) into an end-to-end guarantee.
theorems.proof_sketch: Acyclic case: T8 normalizes the input delta without changing semantics (T15 ensures no size increase). T7 ensures functorial coherence through composed operators. T1+T2 guarantee propagation correctness. T4 guarantees optimal planning. Composition yields exact repair. Cyclic case: T9 guarantees fixed-point existence and convergence within diam(G)·h iterations. T12 guarantees widening soundness with 1.2× precision. T7 ensures coherence is preserved through cycles. End-to-end: repair output ⊒ exact output (soundness) and cost ≤ 1.2·OPT (near-optimality).
key_lemmas: L38: Composition of sound components yields sound system
L39: Precision bounds compose multiplicatively across pipeline stages
theorems.novelty_rating: 7/10 - End-to-end correctness proof composing all extended theorems is the capstone result
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides a single theorem guaranteeing the entire extended ARC system is correct. Users can trust the system without understanding individual component proofs.

theorems.name: Spectral Sensitivity Bound
theorems.statement: For a pipeline with k stages, error amplification through delta propagation is bounded by ρ_k(P)·‖σ‖ where P is the state-dependent propagation matrix at reference state S₀ and ρ_k denotes the k-step spectral radius.
theorems.proof_strategy: Induction on pipeline depth k using sub-multiplicativity of the propagation matrix (L30) and spectral amplification lemma (L31). Bound each stage's contribution via the delta norm (D26).

theorems.name: Expected Cost Concentration
theorems.statement: Under the stochastic perturbation model (D29), the repair cost satisfies a sub-exponential tail bound: P(cost(π) > (1+t)·E[cost(π)]) ≤ exp(-c·min(t², t)·k) for pipeline depth k and constant c depending on the cost function.
theorems.proof_strategy: Apply bounded differences inequality (L32) to the stochastic repair cost (D30). The bounded differences condition follows from the Lipschitz property of the cost function under the delta norm.

theorems.name: Minimax Optimal Repair
theorems.statement: The worst-case optimal repair plan under an uncertainty set U (D31) reduces to computing the optimal plan at the worst-case perturbation: min_π max_{σ∈U} cost(π,σ) = min_π cost(π, σ*) where σ* = argmax_{σ∈U} min_π cost(π,σ).
theorems.proof_strategy: Minimax theorem application exploiting convexity of the cost function in the perturbation and compactness of the uncertainty set. Reduction to a single planning instance at the saddle point.

theorems.name: Quantitative Bisimulation Correctness
theorems.statement: The quantitative repair bisimulation B_ε (D33) satisfies: (1) B_0 is exact for pipelines in Fragment F, (2) B_ε provides computable degradation bounds outside F with ε determined by the frontier set distance, and (3) bisimulation respects compositional rules for sequential and parallel pipeline composition.
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (1) by reduction to T2 (bounded commutation). Part (2) by constructing the bisimulation relation from the repair plan sensitivity metric (D34) and bounding via the frontier set (D35). Part (3) by structural induction on pipeline composition operators.

theorems.name: Differentially Private Repair
theorems.statement: The exponential mechanism over valid repair plans achieves ε-differential privacy while guaranteeing that the selected plan's cost is within O(ln(|Plans|)/ε) of optimal with high probability.
theorems.proof_strategy: Standard exponential mechanism analysis with the cost function as the quality score. Privacy follows from bounded sensitivity of the cost function under adjacent database instances. Utility bound via the exponential mechanism's concentration property.

algorithms.name: PROPAGATE - Delta Propagation Through Pipeline DAG
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM PROPAGATE(G, σ, v_src):
  // Initialize delta map with identity deltas
  Δ := Map<Node, (Δ_S, Δ_D, Δ_Q)>()
  for each v ∈ V do
    Δ[v] := (ε_S, 0_D, ⊥_Q)
  
  // Set source perturbation
  Δ[v_src] := σ
  
  // Topological traversal
  topo_order := TOPOLOGICAL_SORT(G)
  
  for each v ∈ topo_order do
    if v == v_src then continue
    
    // Aggregate incoming deltas from all predecessors
    δ_s_agg, δ_d_agg, δ_q_agg := ε_S, 0_D, ⊥_Q
    
    for each u ∈ PREDECESSORS(v) do
      (δ_s_u, δ_d_u, δ_q_u) := Δ[u]
      
      // Apply interaction homomorphisms before aggregation
      δ_d_transformed := φ(δ_s_u)(δ_d_u)
      δ_q_transformed := ψ(δ_s_u)(δ_q_u)
      
      // Aggregate using sort-specific operations
      δ_s_agg := δ_s_agg ∘ δ_s_u        // Monoid composition
      δ_d_agg := δ_d_agg + δ_d_transformed  // Group addition
      δ_q_agg := δ_q_agg ⊔ δ_q_transformed  // Lattice join
    
    // Push through operator at node v
    f_v := OPERATOR(v)
    δ_s_out := PUSH_SCHEMA(f_v, δ_s_agg)
    δ_d_out := PUSH_DATA(f_v, δ_d_agg)
    δ_q_out := PUSH_QUALITY(f_v, δ_q_agg)
    
    Δ[v] := (δ_s_out, δ_d_out, δ_q_out)
  
  return Δ
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G = (V, E, λ, τ), compound perturbation σ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q), source node v_src ∈ V
algorithms.output: Map Δ: V → (Δ_S × Δ_D × Δ_Q) where Δ[v] is the propagated delta at node v
algorithms.correctness: Hexagonal coherence (T1) ensures interaction homomorphisms preserve operator semantics. Topological order guarantees all dependencies are processed before dependent nodes
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|V| + |E| + |V|·|C|·|Δ|) where |C| is average schema size and |Δ| is delta representation size
algorithms.space_complexity: O(|V|·|C|·|Δ|) for delta storage at each node

algorithms.name: COMPOSE - Compound Perturbation Composition
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM COMPOSE(σ₁, σ₂):
  // Extract components
  (δ_s1, δ_d1, δ_q1) := σ₁
  (δ_s2, δ_d2, δ_q2) := σ₂
  
  // Schema composition (monoid operation)
  δ_s_composed := COMPOSE_SCHEMA_DELTAS(δ_s1, δ_s2)
  
  // Data composition with interaction homomorphism
  δ_d_transformed := APPLY_INTERACTION_PHI(δ_s1, δ_d2)
  δ_d_composed := GROUP_ADD(δ_d1, δ_d_transformed)
  
  // Quality composition with interaction homomorphism  
  δ_q_transformed := APPLY_INTERACTION_PSI(δ_s1, δ_q2)
  δ_q_composed := LATTICE_JOIN(δ_q1, δ_q_transformed)
  
  return (δ_s_composed, δ_d_composed, δ_q_composed)

SUBROUTINE COMPOSE_SCHEMA_DELTAS(δ_s1, δ_s2):
  // Apply δ_s1 then δ_s2 with conflict resolution
  result := []
  
  for each op1 ∈ δ_s1 do
    result.append(op1)
  
  for each op2 ∈ δ_s2 do
    if CONFLICTS_WITH_PRIOR(op2, result):
      result := RESOLVE_CONFLICT(result, op2)
    else:
      result.append(op2)
  
  return result

SUBROUTINE APPLY_INTERACTION_PHI(δ_s, δ_d):
  // Transform data delta through schema homomorphism
  for each schema_op ∈ δ_s do
    δ_d := APPLY_SCHEMA_TO_DATA(schema_op, δ_d)
  return δ_d

SUBROUTINE APPLY_INTERACTION_PSI(δ_s, δ_q):
  // Transform quality delta through schema homomorphism
  for each schema_op ∈ δ_s do
    δ_q := APPLY_SCHEMA_TO_QUALITY(schema_op, δ_q)
  return δ_q
algorithms.input: Two compound perturbations σ₁ = (δ_s1, δ_d1, δ_q1), σ₂ = (δ_s2, δ_d2, δ_q2)
algorithms.output: Composed perturbation σ₁ ∘ σ₂ with interaction effects
algorithms.correctness: Associativity proven by T6. Interaction homomorphisms preserve cross-sort dependencies
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|δ_s1|·|δ_s2| + |δ_d|·|C| + |δ_q|·|Q|) for conflict resolution and interaction application

algorithms.name: EXECUTE-REPAIR - Repair Plan Execution with Checkpointing
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM EXECUTE_REPAIR(G, repair_plan, checkpoint_manager):
  actions := repair_plan.actions
  execution_order := TOPOLOGICAL_SORT(EXTRACT_NODES(actions))
  
  // Create execution checkpoint
  checkpoint_id := checkpoint_manager.CREATE_CHECKPOINT()
  
  try:
    for each node ∈ execution_order do
      if RECOMPUTE(node) ∈ actions:
        
        // Pre-execution validation
        if not VALIDATE_NODE_PRECONDITIONS(node):
          throw REPAIR_EXECUTION_ERROR(f"Preconditions failed for {node}")
        
        // Execute recomputation
        LOG(INFO, f"Recomputing node {node}")
        start_time := NOW()
        
        query := GET_QUERY(node)
        input_tables := GET_INPUT_TABLES(node)
        output_table := GET_OUTPUT_TABLE(node)
        
        # Execute SQL with timeout protection
        result := EXECUTE_SQL_WITH_TIMEOUT(query, input_tables, TIMEOUT_SECONDS)
        
        # Atomic table replacement
        temp_table := CREATE_TEMP_TABLE(output_table)
        INSERT_INTO(temp_table, result)
        VALIDATE_OUTPUT_SCHEMA(temp_table, EXPECTED_SCHEMA(node))
        ATOMIC_SWAP(output_table, temp_table)
        
        execution_time := NOW() - start_time
        LOG(INFO, f"Node {node} recomputed in {execution_time}ms")
        
        # Update repair statistics
        repair_plan.record_execution(node, execution_time, len(result))
    
    # Validation phase: verify repair correctness
    if FRAGMENT_F_PIPELINE(G):
      # Perfect correctness expected
      validation_result := VALIDATE_PERFECT_REPAIR(G, repair_plan)
      if not validation_result.correct:
        throw REPAIR_CORRECTNESS_ERROR(validation_result.error_msg)
    else:
      # Bounded correctness: check error bounds
      deviation := COMPUTE_REPAIR_DEVIATION(G, repair_plan)
      error_bound := COMPUTE_ERROR_BOUND(G, repair_plan.perturbation)
      if deviation > error_bound:
        throw REPAIR_CORRECTNESS_ERROR(f"Deviation {deviation} exceeds bound {error_bound}")
    
    # Commit checkpoint
    checkpoint_manager.COMMIT_CHECKPOINT(checkpoint_id)
    
    return REPAIR_SUCCESS(
      nodes_recomputed=len(execution_order),
      total_time=SUM(repair_plan.execution_times),
      correctness_guarantee=FRAGMENT_F_PIPELINE(G) ? "PERFECT" : f"BOUNDED(ε={error_bound})"
    )
  
  except Exception as e:
    # Rollback on any failure
    LOG(ERROR, f"Repair execution failed: {e}")
    checkpoint_manager.ROLLBACK_CHECKPOINT(checkpoint_id)
    return REPAIR_FAILURE(error=str(e), checkpoint_restored=true)

SUBROUTINE VALIDATE_PERFECT_REPAIR(G, repair_plan):
  # For Fragment F pipelines: repair result must equal full recomputation
  perturbation := repair_plan.perturbation
  
  # Execute full recomputation as ground truth
  G_evolved := EVOLVE_PIPELINE(G, perturbation)
  truth_results := EXECUTE_FULL_PIPELINE(G_evolved)
  
  # Compare with repair results
  repair_results := EXTRACT_CURRENT_RESULTS(G)
  
  for each output_table ∈ GET_OUTPUT_TABLES(G) do
    truth_data := truth_results[output_table]
    repair_data := repair_results[output_table]
    
    if not MULTISET_EQUAL(truth_data, repair_data):
      return VALIDATION_FAILURE(f"Mismatch in {output_table}: expected {len(truth_data)} rows, got {len(repair_data)}")
  
  return VALIDATION_SUCCESS()
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G, repair plan with recomputation actions, checkpoint manager for rollback
algorithms.output: Repair execution result with correctness guarantees and performance statistics
algorithms.correctness: Atomic execution with checkpointing ensures all-or-nothing semantics. Validation against theoretical guarantees (perfect for Fragment F, bounded otherwise)
algorithms.time_complexity: O(Σ_{v∈repair_actions} COST(v)) - sum of recomputation costs plus validation overhead

algorithms.name: Stream-Propagate - Streaming Delta Propagation
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM STREAM_PROPAGATE(stream, G, merge_order):
  // Streaming propagation with canonical merge order
  buffer := empty_delta_map()
  for each (v, δ) in stream do
    buffer[v] := MERGE(buffer[v], δ, merge_order)  // canonical order
    for each u ∈ downstream(v, G) do
      δ_prop := push_{v→u}(buffer[v])
      buffer[u] := MERGE(buffer[u], δ_prop, merge_order)
    if FLUSH_CONDITION(buffer) then
      EMIT(buffer)
      buffer := empty_delta_map()
  EMIT(buffer)  // final flush
algorithms.complexity: O(|V|·k·|Δ|) time where k is pipeline depth and |Δ| is delta size
algorithms.correctness: Equivalent to batch propagation under canonical merge order (D41). Proven via streaming-batch equivalence (P28).

algorithms.name: Adaptive-Repair - Online Adaptive Repair Planning
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM ADAPTIVE_REPAIR(G, σ_stream):
  // Online repair with exponential threshold
  demand := zero_counters()  // D37
  τ := τ₀  // initial threshold
  for each σ_t in σ_stream do
    affected := PROPAGATE(G, σ_t)
    for each v ∈ affected do
      demand[v] += 1
      if demand[v] ≥ τ then
        RECOMPUTE(v)  // full recomputation
        demand[v] := 0
      else
        INCREMENTAL_REPAIR(v, σ_t)  // incremental
    τ := τ₀ · 2^{⌊log₂(t)⌋}  // exponential threshold (D42)
algorithms.complexity: O(log k) competitive ratio where k is the number of perturbation stages
algorithms.correctness: Competitive ratio proven via potential function argument. Threshold schedule ensures logarithmic regret.
